title: What are Differential Equations
subtitle: Builds the core mental model: equations whose unknowns are functions, and why they describe the changing world.
description: Builds the core mental model: equations whose unknowns are functions, and why they describe the changing world.

atAGlance.title: Unit 0 at a Glance: Core Sub-Topics
atAGlance.summary: This opening unit builds the core mental model of the whole course: a differential equation is an equation whose unknown is a \emph{function}, tied to its own rates of change. Each sub-topic below sharpens that idea, moving from raw definition toward why these equations describe the changing world.

bullets.body: the unknown is a function, and the equation relates it to its own derivatives.
what it means for a function to be a solution, and why one equation yields a whole family of curves.
the vocabulary of order, linearity, ODE versus PDE, and the slope-field picture of a rate law.
how laws about rates and forces become equations we can analyze, from growth to cooling.

modules.title: What are Differential Equations
modules.topics: A \emph{differential equation} is an equation whose unknown is a function and which relates that function to one or more of its own derivatives. This is the one structural fact that separates it from algebra --- in algebra you solve for a number, but here you solve for a whole function. The \emph{order} of the equation is simply the highest derivative that appears.

formulas.title: Definition and Order
formulas.body:
A differential equation links an unknown function $y(x)$ to its derivatives:
\[
    F\!\left(x,\; y,\; \frac{dy}{dx},\; \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}},\; \dots\right) = 0.
\]
The \strong{order} is the highest derivative present. For example,
$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 3y$ is \emph{first order}, while $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + 4y = 0$
is \emph{second order}.

examples.title: Worked Example 0.1: Read $\frac{dy}{dx} = 3y$
examples.setup:
\strong{Unknown.} The function $y(x)$, not the constant $3$ and not the input $x$.\\
\strong{Order.} The highest derivative is the first derivative, so the equation
is first order.\\
\strong{Reading.} It says: find every function whose rate of change equals three
times its own current value.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: The Unknown Is a Function, Not a Number
warning.body:
The most common first-week mistake is hunting for a single number, as in
algebra. The unknown here is an entire function $y(x)$ --- a rule defined across
its whole domain. A solution is satisfied at \emph{every} point, not at one
isolated value.

modules.title: How Differential Equations Work
modules.topics: To \emph{solve} a differential equation is to find the function --- or family of functions --- that satisfies it for all inputs. You \emph{check} a candidate by substituting it and its derivatives back in and confirming both sides agree everywhere. Because a first-order equation fixes only the slope at each point and not the starting height, its general solution carries one arbitrary constant, and an initial condition selects one curve from that family.

formulas.title: Solution, General versus Particular
formulas.body:
A function is a solution when substitution makes the equation hold for all $x$.
The \strong{general solution} of a first-order equation carries one free
constant; an \strong{initial condition} $y(x_0) = y_0$ pins it down:
\[
    y = F(x) + C
    \;\;\xrightarrow{\;y(x_0)=y_0\;}\;\;
    \text{one particular curve.}
\]

methodology.title: Algorithm: Verifying a Candidate Solution
steps: Compute the derivatives of the candidate that the equation requires.
Substitute the candidate and those derivatives into the equation.
Simplify the left-hand side and the right-hand side independently.
Confirm the two sides are equal as an identity in $x$ --- true for every input, not just one point.

examples.title: Worked Example 0.2: Verify $y = e^{2x}$ solves $\frac{dy}{dx} = 2y$
examples.setup:
\strong{Step 1.} Differentiate the candidate:
\[ \frac{dy}{dx} = 2e^{2x}. \]
\strong{Step 2.} Compute the right side from the candidate:
\[ 2y = 2e^{2x}. \]
\strong{Step 3.} Both sides equal $2e^{2x}$ for all $x$, so $y = e^{2x}$ is a
solution.

modules.title: A Tourist's Guide to Differential Equations
modules.topics: Before solving anything, you learn to classify. An equation is an \emph{ordinary} differential equation (ODE) when its unknown depends on a single independent variable, and \emph{partial} (PDE) when it depends on several. It is \emph{linear} when the unknown and its derivatives appear only to the first power and are never multiplied together. Geometrically, a first-order equation is a \emph{slope field} --- a direction mark at every point that solution curves must follow.

formulas.title: Classification Vocabulary
formulas.body:
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em, itemsep=2pt]
    \item \strong{Order} --- the highest derivative present.
    \item \strong{ODE vs PDE} --- one independent variable versus several.
    \item \strong{Linear} --- $y$ and its derivatives appear to the first power
          only, with no products such as $y\,y'$ and no terms like $\sin y$.
    \item \strong{Slope field} --- the picture $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x,y)$ paints,
          assigning a direction to every point of the plane.
\end{itemize}

examples.title: Worked Example 0.3: Classify three equations
examples.setup:
\strong{(a)} $\dfrac{dy}{dx} + y = x$ --- first order, linear, ODE.\\
\strong{(b)} $\dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}} + \sin\theta = 0$ --- second order,
\emph{nonlinear} (the $\sin\theta$ term), ODE.\\
\strong{(c)} $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}}$
--- a PDE, since $u$ depends on both $t$ and $x$.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Order Is Not Term Count or Degree
warning.body:
The order is set \emph{only} by the highest derivative --- not by the number of
terms, the size of the coefficients, or the power to which the function is
raised. The equation $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^{3} = y$ is still
\emph{first order}; the cube is a degree, not an order.

modules.title: Why You Are Learning Differential Equations
modules.topics: Differential equations are the language of science because most physical laws describe \emph{how} a quantity changes, which is exactly what a derivative measures. To model a phenomenon you identify the changing quantity and what its rate of change depends on. The same handful of rate laws --- a rate proportional to an amount, or to a difference --- reappear across growth, decay, and cooling.

formulas.title: Standard Models
formulas.body:
A rate proportional to the current amount gives exponential behavior:
\[
    \frac{dP}{dt} = kP \;\Longrightarrow\; P(t) = P_{0}e^{kt}
    \qquad (k>0 \text{ grows},\; k<0 \text{ decays}).
\]
Radioactive decay and Newton's law of cooling share the structure:
\[
    \frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N,
    \qquad
    \frac{dT}{dt} = -k\,(T - T_{s}).
\]
Newton's second law writes acceleration as a second derivative of position:
$F = m\dfrac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}}$.

examples.title: Worked Example 0.4: Set up a growth model
examples.setup:
\strong{Step 1.} A population whose growth rate is proportional to its size
obeys
\[ \frac{dP}{dt} = kP. \]
\strong{Step 2.} Check that $P(t) = P_{0}e^{kt}$ fits: its derivative is
$kP_{0}e^{kt} = kP$, matching the equation, and $P(0) = P_{0}$ sets the initial
size. The single rate law captures the whole exponential family.

cheat.subtitle: Condensed Cheat Sheet: What are Differential Equations

takeaway.title: Unit 0 Key Takeaway
takeaway.body:
The whole unit rests on one shift in viewpoint --- the unknown is a
\highlight{function}, and the equation pins down its
\highlight{rate of change}. You \highlight{verify} a
candidate by substituting it and its derivatives and checking both sides agree
for all $x$, and you pass from the \highlight{general solution} to
one particular curve with an \highlight{initial condition}. Read
the structure first --- order, linearity, and what is changing --- and every
later technique in the course has a place to land.
